CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Mar-12-2025 Off Calendar

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Sep-11-2024 Continued To Apr-09-25 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION FOR JOINDER OF ADDITIONAL PARTIES
Off Calendar Aug-15-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KINTETSU WORLD EXPRESS (U.S.A.), INC.'s MOTION FOR JOINDER OF ADDITIONAL PARTIES.

The July 29, 2024, joinder of KINTETSU WORLD EXPRESS is unopposed and granted.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis C. Karnow; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Individual Arbitration And Stay Action
Off Calendar Aug-15-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT EMPLOYBRIDGE HOLDING COMPANY, EMPLOYBRIDGE, LLC AND REAL TIME STAFFING SERVICES, INC. D/B/A SELECT's Motion To Compel Individual Arbitration And Stay Action.

Granted.

The parties failed to provide courtesy copies. Local Rule 2.7B. Counsel are admonished, and warned that violations of local rules may be the subject of sanctions.

Plaintiff Cruz resists the motion to compel arbitration on essentially two grounds: first, that the arbitration agreement violates his "7th amendment constitutional right to a trial by jury," Opp. at 2:1, and second, the agreement is unconscionable.

The 7th Amendment to the US Constitution does not apply to the states. Entin v. Superior Ct., 208 Cal. App. 4th 770, 778 &n.4 (2012); Nationwide Biweekly Admin., Inc. v. Superior Ct. of Alameda Cnty., 9 Cal. 5th 279, 330 (2020). Of course, California has its own constitutional (and sometimes statutory) rights to a jury, but even if Cruz means to invoke those, he presents no argument on the matter. And as the defense has established, he was offered the choice to agree to arbitration, or not, and voluntarily chose arbitration. There is no reason to set aside his obvious, uncontested intentional act in waiving his right to a jury.

As to unconscionability, Cruz has no evidence to support any of the arguments in the Opposition, and so the factual recitations in that Opposition are valueless. This alone warrants granting the motion.

Turning to the merits in any event: as Cruz agrees, both procedural and substantive unconscionability must be shown. The only argument on procedural unconscionability is that the agreement is one of adhesion. But it is undisputed that the arbitration agreement, specifically, was voluntary and played no role in whether he could be enrolled as employee. There is no evidence for any of the other arguments made by Cruz (time to review agreement, pressure to sign, etc.). Although the Opposition says the agreement's language was complex, none is cited.

Because there is no procedural unconscionability, the analysis comes to an end and there can be no overall finding of unconscionability. Ramirez v. Charter Commc'ns, Inc., __Cal.5th __, 551 P.3d 520, 531 (2024).

However, even if we examine substantive unconscionability, there is but one argument made by Cruz: that there were no rules for the arbitration appended or linked-to in the agreement. But there are no pertinent rules to append. The agreement is simply that the parties will select an arbiter; presumably, the parties and the arbiter will then devise a plan for the arbitration. There's nothing missing from the Agreement, and the defendants didn't hide anything from Cruz.

The motion is granted. The case is referred to arbitration and this Superior Court matter is stayed.

Order is signed.

Judge: Curtis C. Karnow; Clerk: W. Trupek; Not Reported (302/RBU)
